title: Today's Tech Brief: The Pope, AI, and a Dash of Irony
summary: The Pope's recent encyclical on artificial intelligence has raised eyebrows, with some parts seemingly written by AI itself. I'm diving into the story, exploring the Vatican's unexpected alliance with Silicon Valley, and what this means for the power of AI.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Did the Pope use AI to write about the dangers of AI?
imageCaption: Did the Pope use AI to write about the dangers of AI? (theverge.com)

body.text: As I delve deeper into the story of the Pope's encyclical on artificial intelligence, I'm struck by the sheer irony of it all. It appears that parts of the document, Magnifica Humanitas, may have been written by AI itself. This has raised more than a few eyebrows, given the Pope's warnings about the potential risks of relying too heavily on artificial intelligence. I mean, who wouldn't want to read about the dangers of AI, potentially penned by the very technology being warned about? It's like the ultimate plot twist.

According to reports from The Verge, analyses have determined that certain sections of the encyclical bear the hallmarks of AI-generated text. This has sparked a lively debate about the role of AI in shaping our understanding of the world. On one hand, it's fascinating to see the Church embracing AI as a tool for exploring complex issues. On the other hand, it's a bit unsettling to think that the very technology being warned about might be influencing the narrative. As I see it, this is a classic case of 'the medium is the message' - the fact that AI might have been used to write about its own dangers says a lot about the complexities of our relationship with technology.

But that's not all - the Vatican has also been making some interesting alliances in the tech world. Christopher Olah, co-founder of Anthropic, was invited to speak at the presentation of the Pope's encyclical. This signals a significant partnership between the Catholic Church and Silicon Valley, and it's got people wondering what this might mean for the future of AI development. As I see it, this is a clear indication that the Church is taking a proactive approach to understanding and shaping the role of AI in our lives. It's almost as if the Vatican is saying, 'if you can't beat 'em, join 'em' - and who can blame them? The tech world is moving at a breakneck pace, and it's only natural that the Church would want to stay ahead of the curve.

Wired has been digging deeper into the encyclical itself, and what it says about the power of AI. The document highlights the ways in which algorithms are increasingly shaping our lives - from what we see and read, to the processes that govern our work and collective choices. It's a sobering reminder of just how pervasive AI has become, and the need for thoughtful consideration about its impact on our world. As I read through the encyclical, I'm struck by the sheer scope of AI's influence. It's not just about automation or efficiency - it's about the very fabric of our society. And that's what makes this story so fascinating - the intersection of technology, faith, and humanity.

Did the Pope use AI to write about the dangers of AI?, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: AI News Did the Pope use AI to write about the dangers of AI? Analyses have determined that parts of the Magnifica Humanitas appear to have been written by AI. Analyses have determined that parts of the Magnifica Humanitas appear to have been written by AI. by Jay Peters and Hayden Field May 27, 2026, 12:38 AM UTC Getty Images It’s possible that AI was used... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.
